title: Common Context Engineering Mistakes
concept: Dumping entire documents, ignoring token budgets, and burying the actual user question.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what's the difference between static context and dynamic context?

options.text: Static context is fixed and reused, dynamic context is reassembled fresh for each request
Static context uses images, dynamic context uses text only
There's no real difference, they're the same thing

exercises.explain: Right! Static context (like a system prompt) stays the same every time, while dynamic context gets rebuilt fresh based on the current request — like retrieved documents or recent chat history.

cards.title: More Context = Better, Right?
cards.body: You'd think stuffing every possible document into an LLM's context would make it smarter. Instead, it often gets confused, slow, and expensive — and sometimes flat-out misses the point of the question.

cards.title: Three Classic Context Mistakes
cards.body: 1) Dumping entire documents instead of relevant snippets. 2) Ignoring token budgets so context overflows or gets truncated. 3) Burying the actual user question under piles of background info so the model loses track of what it's supposed to answer.

cards.title: Packing for a Weekend Trip
cards.body: Good context engineering is like packing a carry-on: you bring exactly what you need. Dumping whole documents is like packing your entire closet — you'll never fit it, and you can't find your socks. The question getting buried is like your passport ending up at the bottom of an overstuffed suitcase.

cards.title: The Overstuffed Support Bot
cards.body: A support bot pastes an entire 40-page manual into every prompt, then asks 'How do I reset my password?' at the very end. The model spends its attention wading through unrelated sections and sometimes answers a different question from a nearby paragraph instead.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Context Window Means No Limits
cards.body: Even with huge context windows, more tokens mean higher cost, slower responses, and more chances for the model's attention to get diluted. A model with a 200k-token window can still perform worse when you cram in 190k tokens of irrelevant text.

cards.title: Sloppy vs Careful Context
cards.body: The same question gets very different answers depending on how the context around it is built.

left.text: Whole document pasted in, question tucked in at the end, tokens way over budget, model gets distracted or truncates the important part

right.text: Only relevant snippets included, question placed clearly near the top, token count checked and trimmed, model answers precisely

exercises.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the common context engineering mistakes covered in this lesson?

options.text: Dumping entire documents instead of relevant excerpts
Ignoring token budgets until context overflows
Burying the user's actual question under background info
Using a temperature setting of 0 for factual answers

exercises.explain: Temperature settings are a separate model parameter, not a context engineering mistake. The three mistakes this lesson covers are dumping whole documents, ignoring token budgets, and burying the real question.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model has a very large context window, you never need to worry about how much text you include.
exercises.explain: False. Even large context windows have costs — more tokens mean higher price, slower responses, and diluted attention. Being selective still matters no matter how big the window is.

exercises.sentence: Placing the user's question at the very end of a huge wall of text risks burying the actual question so the model loses track of what to answer.
exercises.explain: Burying the question means hiding it under so much other text that the model can't easily find or prioritize it — one of the classic context mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Which of these context-building approaches was most likely generated carelessly by an AI system without human review, based on the mistakes we covered?

options.text: A prompt that includes the top 3 relevant snippets, states the user's question clearly at the start, and stays under the token limit
A prompt that pastes the entire 50-page document, adds unrelated chat history, and puts the user's actual question in the last line
A prompt that summarizes only the sections relevant to the question and repeats the question right before asking for an answer

exercises.explain: Option B shows all three mistakes at once: dumping a whole document, ignoring the token budget by adding irrelevant history, and burying the question at the end — a telltale sign of unreviewed, careless context assembly.

exercises.prompt: You're reviewing a teammate's context engineering pipeline. It retrieves 15 full documents (way over the token limit), concatenates them in random order, and appends the user's question as the very last line. What's the BEST fix, combining everything you've learned?

options.text: Just switch to a model with an even bigger context window so all 15 documents fit
Retrieve only the most relevant snippets, keep total tokens within budget, and place the user's question prominently near the start (or repeat it near the end)
Remove the user's question entirely since the documents already contain the answer somewhere
Keep all 15 documents but ask the model to summarize them first every single time

exercises.explain: The best fix tackles all three mistakes at once: trim to relevant snippets (not whole documents), respect the token budget so nothing gets truncated or diluted, and make sure the actual question is clearly visible and prioritized rather than buried at the end. A bigger window alone doesn't fix irrelevant clutter or a buried question — good context engineering is about being selective and deliberate, not just having more room. You've completed the LLM Application Engineering phase — great work mastering context engineering foundations!
